item.title: Éric Rohmer
item.description: Movement: French New Wave Branch: Cahiers du cinéma Éric Rohmer is the New Wave’s quietest revolutionary.

item.title: François Truffaut
item.description: Movement: French New Wave Branch: Cahiers du cinéma François Truffaut is the New Wave’s warmest director, and maybe its most deceptive.

item.title: Agnès Varda
item.description: Movement: French New Wave Branch: Left Bank Agnès Varda came to cinema from photography and art history, not film criticism, and it shows.

item.title: (1957) Wild Strawberries
item.description: Original title: Smultronstället Director: Ingmar Bergman Movement: Scandinavian Existential Cinema Professor Isak Borg, seventy-eight years old, is driving from Stockholm to Lund to receive an honorary degree.

item.title: (1964) A Hard Day's Night (film)
item.description: Director: Richard Lester Influence: French New Wave Four musicians are chased by fans, ride a train, evade their manager, wander off, get into trouble, play a concert, and escape again.

item.title: (1963) Winter Light
item.description: Original title: Nattvardsgästerna Director: Ingmar Bergman Movement: Scandinavian Existential Cinema Pastor Tomas Ericsson conducts communion in a nearly empty church in rural Sweden.

item.title: Wall of Sound
item.description: Origin: Phil Spector, Gold Star Studios, Hollywood, California, c.

item.title: Backbeat
item.description: Also known as: the backbeat, snare on two and four First heard in: African American gospel and blues of the early twentieth century; codified as the defining rhythmic feature of popular music on 1940s–50s New Orleans R&B recordings by drummer Earl Palmer A Motown record comes on the radio and wi...

item.title: Call and response
item.description: Also known as: antiphony, lead-and-response, the preacher and the congregation First heard in: West African communal music, carried across the Atlantic through work songs, ring shouts, and field hollers; crystallized in African American gospel and spirituals in the nineteenth and early twentieth cen...

item.title: Twelve-bar blues
item.description: Also known as: 12-bar blues, the blues form, the I–IV–V blues First heard in: African American folk blues of the Mississippi Delta and the urban South, early twentieth century; crystallized on record with the 1920s classic blues singers Twelve seconds of Chuck Berry’s “Johnny B.
